title: Brownian motion: non-equilibrium states from equilibrium trajectories -- recovering hydrodynamic regimes from prepared displacement measurements
authors: Jason Boynewicz, Michael C. Thumann, Giuseppe Procopio, Massimiliano Giona
abstract: Owing to the Chapman-Kolmogorov equation for Markovian dynamics,any equilibrium trajectory of a Brownian particle in a solvent fluid can be viewed as the superposition of an uncountable number of non-equilibrium states. This property permits the unraveling of fine details of fluid-particle interactions at microscales defined by its non-equilibrium properties from the analysis of a single Brownian trajectory and to connect them to the hydrodynamics of the solvent fluid, simply considering the lower-order (second) moments of particle position in trapped conditions. In this way, the acceleration due to thermal-hydrodynamic fluctuational forces is isolated from the other factors and the short-time displacement statistics is completely determined by the correlation properties of the fluctuational thermal-hydrodynamic force. This approach not only confirms the $t^{5/2}$-law obtained by Boynewicz et al. (2026), related to fluid inertial effects, but indicates that this scaling may be superseded by a $t^4$-scaling at very short times once the correlated nature of the stochastic forcings is taken into account. The latter result is related to the regularity properties of particle velocity realizations.
summary: This work develops a theoretical framework to extract microscopic fluid-particle interaction details from single Brownian trajectories by treating equilibrium states as superpositions of non-equilibrium paths. It analyzes the short-time scaling of the mean-squared displacement, predicting crossovers between $t^{5/2}$ and $t^4$ scaling depending on the inclusion of correlated stochastic forces. This provides a powerful method to test hydrodynamic models using single-trajectory measurements.
main_problem: The paper aims to unravel fine details of fluid-particle interactions at microscopic scales by analyzing non-equilibrium states derived from single Brownian trajectories, connecting them to macroscopic hydrodynamics.
main_result: The analysis confirms the $t^{5/2}$-law for short-time displacement statistics due to fluid inertial effects but predicts a crossover to a $t^4$-scaling at very short times when correlated stochastic forcings are properly accounted for.
method: The approach utilizes the Chapman-Kolmogorov equation, viewing equilibrium trajectories as superpositions of non-equilibrium states, and analyzing the second-order moments of particle position via Generalized Langevin Equations (GLEs).
model_or_system: The system is a Brownian particle moving in a solvent fluid, modeled using concepts from Markovian dynamics, path integrals, and memory kernels derived from hydrodynamic theory.
key_observables: Mean square displacement $\langle x^2(t) angle$, velocity autocorrelation function, and scaling exponents ($\phi, \zeta, d_v$).
important_parameters: Short time $t$, fluid viscosity ($\eta$), and parameters related to fluid inertia and correlation time.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the Markovian embedding for non-Markovian processes and involves approximations in the Laplace domain for short-time limits.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the mean-squared displacement $m_{xx}(t)$ vs $t$ for various models, showing crossovers between $t^3$, $t^{5/2}$, and $t^4$ scaling regimes depending on the physical assumptions.
paper_structure: The paper builds theory by relating equilibrium paths to non-equilibrium states, develops the mathematical framework using GLEs and memory kernels, and concludes by deriving and comparing the short-time scaling laws for $\langle x^2(t) angle$ under different hydrodynamic assumptions.
why_interesting: The rigorous connection between non-equilibrium path measurements and equilibrium hydrodynamic properties is a powerful theoretical tool for characterizing complex many-body dynamics, relevant for understanding dissipation in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
